coredata.dc:description: It is expected that photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide to produce valuable chemicals play an important role in achieving carbon neutrality goals. Photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency is one of the factors determining photocatalytic activity. Herein, carbon dots with oxygen-containing protoporphyrin (PP-CDs) were loaded on g-C3N4 nanosheets form a heterostructure by a facile mechanical stirring method. The g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 has a conduction band potential of −1.31 eV and a bandgap of 2.51 eV, in the absence of any added photosensitizer and sacrificial agent under illumination, the methanol yield achieved 357.5 μmol·g−1·h−1, which is 2.2 times than that of g-C3N4 (162.1 μmol·g−1·h−1), after four cycles, the photocatalytic activity remained unchanged. Based on the experimental and characterizations results, it is indicated that the photocatalytic reduction performance of CO2 to methanol was improved mainly attributed to the visible light absorption and the high Lewis acidity and alkalinity. Furthermore, the steric hindrance was provided for photogenerated electron-hole recombination due to the recombination of carbon points his work offers a promising strategy for constructing efficient photocatalysts to achieve CO2 to CH3OH transformation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is expected that photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide to produce valuable chemicals play an important role in achieving carbon neutrality goals. Photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency is one of the factors determining photocatalytic activity. Herein, carbon dots with oxygen-containing protoporphyrin (PP-CDs) were loaded on g-C3N4 nanosheets form a heterostructure by a facile mechanical stirring method. The g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 has a conduction band potential of −1.31 eV and a bandgap of 2.51 eV, in the absence of any added photosensitizer and sacrificial agent under illumination, the methanol yield achieved 357.5 μmol·g−1·h−1, which is 2.2 times than that of g-C3N4 (162.1 μmol·g−1·h−1), after four cycles, the photocatalytic activity remained unchanged. Based on the experimental and characterizations results, it is indicated that the photocatalytic reduction performance of CO2 to methanol was improved mainly attributed to the visible light absorption and the high Lewis acidity and alkalinity. Furthermore, the steric hindrance was provided for photogenerated electron-hole recombination due to the recombination of carbon points his work offers a promising strategy for constructing efficient photocatalysts to achieve CO2 to CH3OH transformation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the carbon resource recovery and carbon dioxide (CO2) emission reduction have been considered as the intractable issues at a global level [1]. Photocatalytic CO2 conversion is deemed as an attractive pathways to achieve the goal of carbon peak and carbon neutrality [2,3]. Semiconductor materials are capable of absorbing visible light and producing charge carriers under light irradiation. the adsorbed CO2 molecules are converted into valuable products, such as CH4, CO, and CH3OH through a reduction reaction with photo-generated electrons [4]. Nevertheless, semiconductor photocatalysts have been confronted with some defects, including limited light-trapping capability, sluggish surface reaction kinetics, and poor CO2 adsorption activation capacity [5].

Recently, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has considerably captured attentions in the field of CO2 photoreduction because it not only a tunable electronic structure and great chemical stability but is also non-toxic and environmentally friendly [6,7]. However, g-C3N4 has several drawbacks that are unfavorable for CO2, such as poor visible light absorption, a fast photogenerated carrier complexation rate, and weak CO2 adsorption and activation capacity [8]. To overcome these disadvantages, several efforts have been made to design and construct g-C3N4-based photocatalysts to improve the efficiency of CO2 reduction, which including noble metal deposition, heterojunction construction, and surface modification [9–12]. The loading of different semiconductors on the surface of g-C3N4 has been proven to be one of the effective methods. It can significantly accelerate the separation and transfer efficiency of photocarriers and broaden the ability to capture visible light [13]. For instance, Li et al. synthesized a series of Au/g-C3N4 (Au/CN) nanocomposites photocatalyst via constant temperature bath-reduction method. The gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) increased the number of unsaturated dangling bonds on the surface. This factor is beneficial for the effective separation and transmission of photogenerated electrons at the interface between Au NPs and CN, thus the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 has been significantly improved [14]. Although loading noble metal on g-C3N4 can effectively improve the photocatalytic activity, the high price of noble metal results in a high application cost. In addition, toxic or easily oxidizable substances may react with the noble metal catalysts under certain reactions, cause photocatalyst inactivation [15–18].

Carbon dots (CDs), as new and environmentally friendly zero-dimensional nanomaterials with sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm [19,20], have both outstanding electron transfer and electron storage capabilities as well as remarkable up conversion photoluminescence properties [21]. It is reported that the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 performance is significantly improved when carbon dots are coupled with other semiconductor such as g-C3N4, BiVO4, and TiO2 [22–24]. Xu et al. proposed a two-step hydrothermal-calcination tandem synthesis strategy to introduce carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into CN and form ultra-thin CQD/CN nanosheets, which has reduced thickness, amplified surface area, and heightened crystallinity. The integration of CQDs induces structural reconfiguration in CN, resulting in the exposure of abundant built-in electric fields on the surface. This structural transformation also endows CQD/CN with superior optical characteristics, such as attenuated charge recombination and heightened electron exchange efficiency, which is beneficial for accelerating the photocatalysis reduction process and significant improvement in photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance [25]. Liu et al. using a microwave ultrafast synthesis strategy synthesized the red luminescent CQDs (R-CQDs), the R-CQDs exhibiting similar optical and structural properties but varying in surface functional group sites. Model photocatalysts were synthesized by combining R-CQDs with graphitic carbon nitride using a facile coupling technique, and the effects of different functionalized R-CQDs on CO2 reduction were investigated. It was found that the enrichment of pyrrolic-N and oxygen-containing surface groups on carbon dots can effectively increase the Lewis acidity and alkalinity, which is conducive to the activation of CO2 adsorption, thus can improve the performance of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 [26]. Inspired by these reports, carbon points can be used as electron acceptors to receive photogenerated electrons from g-C3N4, and the excellent up-conversion luminescence properties of carbon points can enhance the absorption of visible light,and the Lewis acid-base enhancement of carbon dots has a significant effect on CO2 adsorption and activation. Therefore, loading g-C3N4 surface with carbon dots having oxygen-containing functional groups can effectively enhance its performance in photocatalytic reduction of CO2.

In this paper, g-C3N4/PP-CDs heterojunction were successfully synthesized by loading PP-CDs enriched with oxygen-containing functional groups (protoporphyrin) on the surface of g-C3N4 through hydrothermal and mechanical mixing methods. The PP-CDs contains abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, which significantly increases the Lewis acidity and alkalinity of the composite, which has a significant effect on the adsorption and activation of CO2, g-C3N4/pp-CDs shows excellent photocatalytic performance for the reduction of CO2 under visible light irradiation. We studied the photocatalytic mechanism of g-C3N4/PP-CDs and discussed the role of PP-CDs in the photocatalytic system.

Melamine (mass fraction ≥99 %, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.), citric acid (CA, analytically pure, Tianjin Kermer Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), protoporphyrin (PP, mass fraction ≥95 %, Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.), ethylenediamine (EDA, analytically pure, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), dialysis bag (MW=1000, Union Carbide). The water used in the experiments was deionized water (DI), which was produced by the laboratory. All reagents were purchased without further purification.

Preparation of g-C3N4: the bulk g-C3N4 was prepared by a simple calcination approach based on literatures [15,27]. 7 g of melamine was heated at 550°C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5°C min−1 under a nitrogen atmosphere. After naturally cooled down to room temperature, the g-C3N4 was obtained. The faint yellow product was collected and then ground into powder with an agate mortar for further use.

Preparation of PP-CDs: 1 g of CA, 10 μL of EDA, and 10 mg of PP were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water with stirring until complete dissolution. Thereafter, the above solution was put into a 200 mL PTFE-lined high-pressure reactor following by a 5 h thermal reaction at 180 °C. After being cooled down to room temperature, the supernatant was centrifuged and separated, and the supernatant was dialyzed (MW: 1000) and treated for 48 h. Finally, the carbon-dot powders (PP-CDs) product was obtained by freeze-dried [28,29].

Reparation of g-C3N4/pp-CDs: 0.005, 0.015, 0.025 g pp-CDs and 5 g of g-C3N4 powder were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water with ultrasonicating homogeneously and stirring magnetically for 48 h. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation and dried under vacuum at 60°C for 6 h. The obtained samples were named g-C3N4/PP-CDs-1, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3, and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-5, respectively. To ensure the rigor of the experiment, 5 g of g-C3N4 was taken without adding PP-CDs, and the other operations were identical to the above steps, and named g-C3N4. Scheme 1

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a MiniFlex 600 X-ray diffractometer with a scanning range of 3–90◦. The morphology and microstructure of the catalysts were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6460LV/INCA) and high resolution (HR) transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHI). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Spectrum GX Spectrum GX) for the identification of the structure and composition of chemical substances. The electronic states and interfacial interactions of the catalyst surface elements were assessed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+) using a monochromatic Al Kα source. Measurement of catalyst absorption peaks using a UV-Vis DRS spectrophotometer (Evolution-220). The photoluminescence spectra of the catalysts were determined using a fluorescence spectrometer (PL, LS-55) at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The N2 adsorption-desorption isothermal curves of the catalysts were measured using a specific surface and pore analyser (Beckman LS-230). Before measurement, the catalyst was pretreated by degassing at 220 °C for 6 h. N2 was used as the adsorption medium, and the adsorption and desorption were carried out at −196 °C under liquid nitrogen. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the catalysts were then calculated by BET and BJH methods. CO2 adsorption was determined using a fully automated specific surface and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2020). The NH3-TPD was determined using VDSorb-9Xi Fully Automated Chemisorbent Analyzer, the steps are as follows: 50 mg of catalyst was loaded into a U-shaped reaction tube and pretreated in an argon atmosphere at 200 ℃ for 1 h. After that, the temperature was lowered to room temperature, 10 %H2/Ar (30 mL/min) mixture was cut in, and the temperature was raised to 300 ℃ at a rate of 5 ℃/min and kept for 2 h. The reaction tube was cooled down to room temperature in an argon atmosphere and pretreated in 10 %NH3/Ar (30 mL/min) for 1 h for NH3 adsorption, and then switched to an argon purge for 30 min and then raised to 500 ℃ (5 ℃/min) and kept for 10 min to obtain NH3 adsorption. After that, the sample was cooled to room temperature under argon atmosphere, pretreated with 10 % NH3/Ar (30 mL/min) for 1 h for NH3 adsorption, and then switched to argon purge for 30 min to increase the temperature to 500 ℃ (10 ℃/min), and then kept for 10 min to obtain the NH3-TPD curve [30].

In order to understand the performance of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to CH3OH for each sample, we performed the following operations for each sample under the same experimental environment [31]: 100 mL of NaOH aqueous solution with a concentration of 0.1 mol/L was measured and placed in a custom-made transparent photochemical reactor, and 20 mg of catalyst was weighed and added to the photochemical reactor, with the light source of a 300 W Xe lamp (Aulight CEL-HXF300-T3), Prior to illumination, CO2 gas was introduced into the photocatalytic reactor and bubbled for 30 min. After the light was turned on, 1 mL of the reaction solution was taken at 1 h intervals during the photocatalytic reaction, the total reaction time was 6 h. The collected reaction solution was heated up in a headspace injector and analyzed by gas chromatography (FULI 9790II) using a carrier gas of N2.

The light/dark photocurrent response (i-t curve), Mott-Schottky curve (MS), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the catalysts were measured using an electrochemical workstation, which can be used to characterize the carrier mobility efficiency of the analyzed catalysts. The electrochemical were conducted on an electrochemical work-station (CH Instruments CHI660C). The electrochemical performance was performed in a standard three-electrode system with a platinum sheet as the counter electrode, a calomel electrode as the reference electrode, a catalyst-coated FTO conductive glass as the working electrode, and a 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte. The electrode was prepared as shown below, in which first, the FTO conductive glass was cleaned with acetone, anhydrous ethanol, and deionized water by ultrasonication for 30 min, and then 5 mg of the catalyst was dissolved in a mixture of 950 μL of deionized water and 50 μL of naphthol, and ultrasonication was performed to obtain a uniform photocatalyst slurry. Finally, 0.2 mL of the photocatalyst slurry was uniformly applied to one side of the conductive glass surface with a pipette gun and dried at room temperature, in this experiment, all the electrochemical testing instruments and measuring procedures were set for conventional photocatalytic electrochemical testing.

As shown in Fig. 1a, g-C3N4 exhibits a stronger diffraction peak at 2θ = 28.1° and a weaker diffraction peak at 2θ = 13.2°. Both of those peaks belong to the typical characteristic diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 [32]. The peak at 13.2° is attributed to the interlayer stacking of the crystal faces of the graphite-like crystal structure (002), the peak at 28.1° can be ascribed to the interfacial structural filling motifs of the crystal faces of the tris-s-triazine unit (100). While, the diffraction peak of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-1, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs- is retained well with no obvious change compared with g-C3N4. In addition, the diffraction peak of the g-C3N4 (002) was analyzed by local zoom, as shown in Fig. 1b. the g-C3N4 (002) peaks of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-1, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs were slightly shifted to the lower angle direction (2θ = 27.5°)compared with the initial g-C3N4, which indicated that the interaction of both g-C3N4 and PP-CDs [33]. The diffraction peak intensity of PP-CDs/g-C3N4 at the (002) crystal plane is lower than that of g-C3N4, which may results from the trace loading of PP-CDs hindering the interlayer stacking of g-C3N4 [34].

The microstructure and morphology of as-prepared samples were further confirmed by SEM, TEM, and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). As shown in Fig. 2a, it is found that g-C3N4 mainly shows a lamellar structure with a relatively large size, which is shown as a micron-sized bulk with a relatively smooth surface. The microscopic morphology of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is basically the same as that of g-C3N4 (Fig. 2b), which indicates that the introduction of PP-CDs did not change the morphology of g-C3N4. The loaded PP-CDs could not be observed may due to the small size and homogeneous distribution on the surface of g-C3N4, which could be perceived in TEM image later. It is observed that g-C3N4 exhibits a typical two-dimensional layered structure with a large number of layers, The fringe lattice spacing of 0.337 nm can be indexed to the characteristic (002) plane of the g-C3N4 [27]. A comparison between Figs. 2d and 2e reveals that PP-CDs are uniformly loaded on the surface of g-C3N4 with the average size of the nanoparticles is approximately 3.2±0.04 nm. Moreover, the lattice fringe spacing of 0.316 nm can be corresponded to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2f)[35,36]. As shown in Fig. 2(g-i), all three major elements (C, N, and O) are homogeneously distributed on g-C3N4. Meanwhile, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 exhibits essentially the same results (Fig. 2j-l), except that the amount of elemental O is increased. From the Fig. 2m, the O abundance was calculated to be 0.99 % for g-C3N4, lower than that of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 (2.35 %), which indicated that the PP-CDs carry a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups.

XPS analysis was conducted to investigate the surface composition and chemical states of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 3a shows a wide-scan XPS profiles of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3, and PP-CDs, in which C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s are found, respectively, indicating the main composed of C, N and on the surface of as-prepared samples. And it can be noticed that the O content of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is significantly due to the presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in PP-CDs. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 are shown in Fig. 3b. The binding energies at around 284.8 eV, 286.30 eV, and 287.95 eV assign to C-C, C-O, and N-CN, respectively. Moreover, it is observed that an additional peak at binding energy of 288.42 eV was fitted, which corresponding to the CO species of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 and PP-CDs, indicating exists a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups in PP-CDs, such as carboxyl and carbonyl groups. The high-resolution N 1 s spectra of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 can be deconvolution into three peaks (Fig. 3c) The characteristic peaks at binding energies of 398.5 eV, 399.53 eV, and 401.14 eV correspond to the N-(C)2, N-(C)3, and N-H moieties, respectively. The g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 exhibits a decreased content of N-(C)3 groups, which implies a relatively low crystallinity of the material [37]. This result is in agreement with the XRD analyses, which may be attributed to the interaction between PP-CDs and g-C3N4. In addition, the N-H group of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is decreased, which may due to the amidation reaction occurring between the carboxyl group on the surface of PP-CDs and the amino group on the surface of g-C3N4 [38]. This result also demonstrates that PP-CDs are successfully loaded on the g-C3N4 surface. As shown in Fig. 3d, the O 1 s high-resolution spectra of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 and PP-CDs can be fitted into two peaks with the binding energies around 531.81 eV and 533.3 eV, which correspond to C-O and CO groups, respectively. For the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of g-C3N4, a peak located at 531.81 eV is assigned to C-O, it shows that g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 contains carboxyl group and hydroxyl group. Through XPS analysis, it was found that the amino group in g-C3N4/PP-CDs was reduced, and there was a peak of CO. It can be concluded that g-C3N4/PP-CDs was connected through amidation reaction.

The pore structures of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 were evaluated by an N2 adsorption-desorption device. For the two samples ( Fig. 4a), it exhibits the type IV adsorption-desorption isotherms with H3-type hysteresis returns as the relative pressure P/P0 increases, which is characteristic of mesoporous structured materials, indicating that the pores of the catalysts are predominantly mesoporous (2–50 nm) in structure [39]. The pore size distributions of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 are presented in Fig. 4b. The pore size distribution of catalysts is broad, mainly within the mesopore range of 5–30 nm. The specific surface area (SBET) values of the two samples are listed in Table 1. The maximum SBET of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 (193.895 m2·g–1) is greater than that of g-C3N4 (53.799 m2·g–1), beacuse PP-CDs are zero-dimensional nanomaterials, The size is usually in the range of several nanometers to several tens of nanometers, with a large specific surface area. When PP-CDs are loaded onto g-C₃N₄, PP-CDs can promote the dispersion and reorganization of g-C₃N₄, thereby increasing its specific surface area. Additionally, after a long time of ultrasonic and stirring during the process of PP-CDs being compounded with g-C₃N₄, this will also lead to an increase in the specific surface area of g-C₃N₄. suggesting that SBET may increasing the activity of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is a contributing factor. To further analyze the underlying mechanism of g-C3N4/PP-CDs composite for photocatalytic CO2 reduction, the CO2 adsorption capacity was first tested as shown in Fig. 4c. The CO2 adsorption capacity of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is significantly improved compared to that of g-C3N4, indicating that loading PP-CDs on the g-C3N4 surface can promotes CO2 adsorption. According to the infrared images before and after the adsorption of CO2 by g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3, a diffraction peak appears at 2341 cm−1 of the adsorbed composite material, corresponding to the stretching vibration of OCO (Figure S1). To gain a deeper understanding of the Lewis acid active sites of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3, the NH3 temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) was conducted, as shown in Fig. 4d. g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 has a characteristic Lewis acid absorption peak at 430 °C, which is caused by the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of PP-CDs [40].

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) has the capability to conduct qualitative and quantitative analyses of the functional groups present on material surfaces. As shown in Figs. 5a and 5b, the FT-IR spectra reveal the chemical bonding and polar functional groups in g-C3N4, PP-CDs, and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5), all catalysts can be observed to have the same typical characteristic diffraction peaks as those of g-C3N4. The absorption band at 798 cm−1 can be attributed to the typical stretching vibrations of the tri-s-triazine structural unit in g-C3N4, while the broad absorption band at 3314–3633 cm−1 can be owed to N-H in the amino group [17]. Several strong absorption bands at 1236 cm−1, 1306 cm−1, 1440 cm−1, 1531 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 was detected correspond to typical stretching vibrations of C-N and CN heterocycles[41]. PP-CDs has a strong diffraction peak at 1627 cm−1 and 1347–1385 cm−1, which corresponds to the planar vibration of the carboxyl group and hydroxyl absorption, additionally, PP-CDs have a diffraction peak at 1700 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO [42]. whereas the peak intensity of absorption bands of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5) at 1333–1401 cm−1, 1455 cm−1, 1538 cm−1, 1640 cm−1 are significantly larger and shifted compared to g-C3N4, which may be due to overlap of the planar vibration of carboxyl group and hydroxyl group with the C-N and CN stretching vibration, moreover, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5) has a CO diffraction peak from PP-CDS at 1700 cm−1. It indicates that PP-CDs were successfully loaded on the surface of g-C3N4. Moreover, the FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4/PP-CDs did not change significantly, which suggests that the loading of PP-CDs did not disrupt the CN-conjugated structure of carbon nitride.

The ability of the catalyst to absorb and utilize light is one of the important factors influencing the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst. The light absorption properties of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5) were analyzed by UV–vis DRS spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5c, the intrinsic absorption edges of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-1, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3, and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-5 are 444 nm, 467 nm, 473 nm, and 510 nm, respectively. These visible-light absorptions are caused by the n-π transitions taking place within the triazine ring and electron transfer from N 2p orbitals to C 2p orbitals [43]. The g-C3N4 exhibits significant absorption in the UV region. However, the absorption and utilization of light by g-C3N4 is weak in the visible region. Obviously, the light absorption range of the composites increases with the rising content of PP-CDs. The g-C3N4/PP-CDs-5 absorbs almost the full spectrum of light, and the absorption of the g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5) for visible light is significantly enhanced due to the efficient broad-spectrum absorption of PP-CDs. In general, enhancing the absorption and utilization of visible light by the catalyst can increase the number of photogenerated electrons and holes under visible light illumination, which is crucial for improving the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst in the visible region.

From the Mott-Schottky curves in Fig. 6a, it can be seen that the tangent slopes of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5) are both positive, indicating both of them are n-type semiconductors [44]. The flat-band potentials of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-1, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-5 are −1.24, −1.29, −1.31 and −1.27 eV, respectively. According to the Eq. 1/Csc2=2/Ne0ε0εr(E-EFB-KT/e), it can be seen that the slope of the tangent line of the M-S curve is the current density N, and the flat-band potential EFB can be determined by the intercept on the transverse coordinate [45,46]. For n-type semiconductors, the flat band potential lies 0.1–0.3 eV below the conduction band [16], from which it can be deduced that the conduction-band potentials of the semiconductors are −1.34, −1.39, −1.41 and −1.37 eV, respectively. The forbidden bandwidths of the catalysts were calculated by the Tauc plot method (Fig. 6b), and the forbidden bandwidths of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-1, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-5 are 2.73, 2.62, 2.51 and 1.67 eV, respectively. It can be found that the forbidden bandwidths of the carbon nitride gradually decrease with the increase in the content of pp-CDs, which confirms that pp-CDs can effectively improve the absorption and utilization of visible light by g-C3N4. According to the formula: Eg=EVB-ECB, the valence band potentials of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5) can be deduced to be 1.39 eV, 1.23 eV, 1.10 and 0.30 eV, respectively.

Carrier transfer and separation can be detected by i-t curves and EIS.A larger response intensity of the steady-state photocurrent of the catalyst indicates a stronger photo-induced charge separation ability and carrier transfer [47]. Meanwhile, a smaller arc radius of the electrochemical impedance spectrum of the catalyst indicates lower resistance to charge transfer and the faster migration of charge carriers [48], resulting in higher photocatalytic activity. As depicted in Fig. 7a, for g-C3N4, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-1 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3, the photocurrent response intensities increase sequentially. The intensity of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is almost twice that of g-C3N4. However, the photoelectric response intensity of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-5 is abruptly decreased, which is attributed to excessive PP-CDs loadings that inhibits the transfer and transportation of photogenerated electrons. g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 possesses the highest transient photocurrent response intensity, indicating that the light absorption ability and photoinduced generation of photogenerated carriers are the strongest in g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3. Fig. 7b shows the electrochemical impedance spectra of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-1, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-5. The Nyquist radii of the composites are all reduced compared to g-C3N4, and the Nyquist radius is the smallest for g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3. It implies that g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 has the lowest interfacial charge transfer resistance, that is, the least resistance to the transport of photogenerated electrons, which is conducive to the migration of photogenerated carriers to the surface of the catalyst to participate in the photocatalytic reaction.

PL spectroscopy and time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL) can be employed to determine the key factors for the increased activity of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3. A lower intensity of the emission peak indicate weaker photogenerated charge complexes [49]. As illustrated in Fig. 8a, the fluorescence emission peak of g-C3N4 is located at 475 nm, and its photoluminescence intensity is the highest, indicating that in g-C3N4, the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes is low, the recombination is rapid, and the migration of carriers is slow, which directly results in the poor photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4. However, the photoluminescence intensity of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5) is significantly lower than that of g-C3N4. Among all the samples, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 shows the lowest photoluminescence intensity, which indicates that g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 has the fastest separation rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, which is attributed to the loading of PP-CDs on the surface of g-C3N4, which effectively enhances the photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency of g-C3N4.

The time-resolved transient photoluminescence decay (TRPL) spectra were shown in Fig. 8b, reflecting the fluorescence changes over time [50]. The attenuation of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is faster than that of g-C3N4, with fluorescence lifetimes of 4.63 and 6.37 ns, respectively. It suggests that the photogenerated electron-hole segregation and transfer of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 are much faster, allowing more electrons to participate in the reduction reaction. This is attributed to the fact that PP-CDs are loaded on the surface of g-C3N4. As a result, the photogenerated electrons are rapidly transferred from g-C3N4 to PP-CDs, effectively suppressing the photogenerated electron-hole recombination. ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and mechanism of photocatalytic CO2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the photocatalyst was mainly evaluated by the yield of methanol produced from the reduction of CO2 under visible light, and the effects of different loading of PP-CDs on the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4/PP-CDs were investigated. The cumulative methanol yields of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/PP-CDs-x (x=1, 3, 5) were compared within a period over 6 h. As can be seen in Fig. 9a, the methanol yields of all samples showed an increasing trend over the 6-h reaction period, in which the yield of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 at 6 h could reach 357.5 μmol·g−1·h−1, which is 2.2 times higher than that of the original g-C3N4 (162.1 μmol·g−1·h−1), as shown in Fig. 9b. The photocatalytic performance of the composites showed a tendency of first increasing and then decreasing when the mass fraction of PP-CDs ranged from 0 % to 5 %. The composites demonstrated the strongest photocatalytic performance when the mass fraction of PP-CDs was 3 %. However, as the loading of PP-CDs was further increased, the photocatalytic performance of the composites began to decline because excessive loading of CDs on the surface of g-C3N4 restricts the light absorption of g-C3N4 and covers the photocatalytic active sites of g-C3N4, thereby leading to a decrease in the photocatalytic performance of the composites. As seen from the control in Fig. 9c, it is evident that the photocatalyst, CO2, and light are essential for the reduction of CO2 to CH3OH. Moreover, in the absence of light and CO2, the photocatalyst produces almost no methanol, suggesting that the photocatalyst has excellent stability. Cyclic stability and reusability are important indices for photocatalysts to achieve further practical applications. Therefore, g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 exhibits the best photocatalytic performance, is chosen as the catalyst for methanol production by cyclic reduction of CO2. As shown in Fig. 9d, through four cycles, efficient degradation performance is still exhibited, and the efficiency of the subsequent cyclic action only shows a negligible decrease compared to the initial CO2 reduction.

Following the above experimental tests and analysis of the results, Scheme 2 demonstrates the charge transport mechanism and photocatalytic CO2 reduction mechanism of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3. Specifically, it is described as follows:

The g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is photoexcited to generate photogenerated electrons-holes (shown in Eq. (1)). The electrons on the catalyst surface react with CO2 to form
CO
2

, the holes react with H2O to form H+ and O2 (Eq. (2) - (3)).
CO
2

combines with H+ in the presence of electrons to form CH3OH as shown in Eq. (4). According to the above experiments and analyses, the band gap (Eg) of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is 2.51 eV, and the conduction band (CB) potential is −1.41 eV. According to the reports [51–55], Scheme 2 elaborates on the mechanism of action of the g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 photocatalysts. Due to the construction of PP-CDs and g-C3N4 heterojunction, the composite has formed a good electron transfer path, when g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 is irradiated by light, the photogenerated carriers of g-C3N4 are excited, the photogenerated electrons are rapidly transferred to the surface of PP-CDs to react with CO2 in a reductive reaction to form CH3OH. Meanwhile, the holes of g-C3N4 react with H2O in an oxidation reaction to generate O2. The PP-CDs can effectively inhibit the electron-hole complex and promote the rapid transfer of electrons and holes to the surface of the catalyst to participate in the reduction and oxidation reaction. Consequently, and the performance of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to CH3OH is significantly improved.

In this study, a g-C3N4/PP-CDs heterojunction was constructed by mechanical mixing method, which exhibited good performance in photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to CH3OH. Due to the high Lewis acidity and alkalinity of the g-C3N4/PP-CDs heterojunction, it has good adsorption and activation ability for CO2, and the specific surface area and visible light absorption capacity of the composite material were significantly increased, and the recombination rate of photoelectrons and holes was reduced. Under illumination conditions, the CH3OH yield of g-C3N4/PP-CDs-3 was 357.5 μmol·g−1·h−1, which was 2.2 times that of g-C3N4 (162.1 μmol·g−1·h−1). This study provides an effective approach for developing and synthesizing high-activity, high-selectivity photocatalytic systems for the conversion of CO2 to CH3OH. Further improving the photocatalytic activity will be our focus in the future.
